props.covers: The pure pointer-interaction state machine, alignment/distribution snapping, in-memory clipboard, and edge-pan auto-scroll that sit between raw pointer events and the model-layer reducer.

text.insert: This layer sits between raw pointer events and the model-layer reducer. The drag pipeline in

s; everything downstream of that — the pointer state machine, snapping math, in-memory clipboard, and edge-pan auto-scroll — is plain, DOM-free logic that consumes normalized events and document/selection state and emits typed
s for the model-layer reducer to apply.
The core reducer for this layer is
— a pure pointer-interaction state machine modeled on term-draw's draw-state.ts architecture, with gesture/type vocabulary in
, a normalized pointer event, and a read-only
(document, selection, tool, viewport, and an optional snap resolver hook) and returns the next state plus any
s to dispatch and an ephemeral

to render that frame (drag-select rect, snap/distribution guides, spacing hints, drop target, connector-drag preview, in-place text-edit request, place-tool ghost). No DOM access happens anywhere in this module — it is testable with plain objects.
— no new state kind was added for Connector Mode; it reuses
. Each kind has its own step function dispatched from
. A pointer-down here first honors the connector-tool branch (port/object starts reuse

, every other hit is inert), otherwise it branches on hit-test kind into a resize, connector-endpoint-drag, connector-bend-drag, connector-create, place, pressing, or (for a double-click on an object) an immediate in-place text-edit request (
allows it — pure glyph shapes refuse the editor); a double-click on empty canvas is a no-op since the free-text object type was retired.
. A sub-threshold release resolves as a click (applying a deferred shift-toggle, or clearing selection on empty canvas); crossing the threshold promotes the gesture into
. This is also where a section drag picks up its members: promotion to move expands the dragged id set to the section's recorded parentId descendants (recursively, via
), folded into the same move gesture rather than a separate mechanism. Promotion is also lock-gated: before entering move, every id in the dragged set is checked with
— if any dragged object is itself a locked section, or sits under a parentId-ancestor section with
, the gesture returns to idle instead of promoting.
) — dragging one or more selected objects, handled by
. Each move computes live snap correction (via

if the host supplies one, otherwise zero correction), applies it uniformly across the dragged set, tracks a live drop-target section by running the same projected-bounds overlap resolver the reducer uses at commit time, and dispatches
every frame. On release it commits membership with
, so bounds-based section adoption or release shares the drag's undo entry. See
for the membership predicate. Escape/pointercancel restores
captured before any snap correction ran, so a snap can never leak into cancelled-drag geometry.
deferral (small targets, no click semantics) and commit to the gesture immediately; the anchored opposite edge/corner never moves, and both dimensions clamp to
(48 world units). Only the edges the active handle actually moves are snap-corrected. The handle-down entry path is gated by the same
check — a locked section (either mode), or any object under a locked-
section, refuses resize and the pointer-down falls back to idle.
) — rubber-band selection over empty canvas with the select tool, handled by
. Tracks a normalized bounds rect as the pointer moves; on release, every object intersecting the bounds becomes the selection (or is unioned into it, for a shift-additive drag-select).
. Starts when a creatable tool (one per placeable object type — rectangle through icon, per
's switch) is armed and the pointer goes down. A sub-threshold release creates a default-size object centered at the point; a drag creates an object sized to the normalized,
-clamped dragged rect. Either way the tool reverts to
and the new object becomes the selection; the reducer resolves its parentId from the final placement geometry.

) — reconnecting an existing connection's endpoint, dragging a brand-new connector from one of a selected object's anchor dots, or dragging a selected connector's bend segment. Endpoint and create gestures resolve the live connect-target via
, which calls the ported AFFiNE connection cascade —
— to pick, in priority order, an anchor snap (within 8 view px), a nearest-outline-point snap (within 8 world px), or a coarse inside-the-shape candidate. On release, an endpoint drag dispatches
when dropped on a candidate, or
(create-and-connect as one history entry) when dropped on empty canvas; a bend drag dispatches a single waypoint update or clears waypoints when the drag snaps back to straight.
Anchor-dot connector creation now has two entry surfaces. Select mode remains selection-only and editor/select-tool-only; Connector Mode is the hover-driven exception.
places four 28px hit targets with 10px resting dots, centered 20 screen px outside
so below-glyph text bands count as visual bounds.

is 0.5; below 50% zoom, select-mode dots are hidden and inert, while Connector Mode bypasses the gate. Hover/press expands the dot into the full 28px arrow button, emits the hovered port for quick-connect preview, and select mode deliberately keeps
while the crosshair cursor is restored only for the active connector tool.
Hovering an anchor dot, with no active connector drag, asks
immediately with a pinned source anchor, routed live preview, and forward arrowhead. In Connector Mode, pressing an object body also enters
keeps choosing the facing side; a body click stays a deliberate no-op. Candidate resolution begins only after the 3-world-unit drag threshold, uses topmost-first paint order through

, and excludes the source object; section interiors produce no attach candidate. Release on a candidate adds a connection, release on empty canvas quick-connects at the drop point, and a port sub-threshold click quick-connects at the computed spawn point, duplicates the source object's type/style metadata with blank text, and requests label editing for the predicted new object id.

Endpoint reconnect also starts immediately from an endpoint hit, excluding the unmoved endpoint object while it resolves candidates. Release with no candidate reverts silently; release with a candidate dispatches
. If the endpoint's object, anchor, or relative position changed, existing connector
are cleared so the rerouted connection does not keep stale bend geometry.
Selected connector bends are hit through SVG pills carrying
resolves them before generic connection hits.
for the current routed path, then
, shows routed live preview points, snaps back to straight when within zoom-scaled tolerance, and commits exactly one history update with new waypoints or
Connector label editing enters through double-click as well:
forwards connector double-clicks to the stage callback, which calls
and opens the routed label editor instead of treating the double-click as a drag gesture.
Connector Mode is a tool-gated branch inside
, not a new state kind. Port hits enter
; object-body hits enter the same gesture with no

Non-reachable hits stay suppressed in the branch: empty canvas, connector endpoints, bend pills, existing connector paths, handles, and double-clicks are inert while the tool is active. Section interiors remain free canvas for connection purposes, matching the routing cascade's section-inside exclusion.
Stickiness lives in reducer tool state: connector-create never dispatches
, so the tool remains armed across completed lines and quick-connect chains. Escape is handled above the machine in
: cancel an in-flight gesture first, otherwise switch the connector tool back to select, otherwise fall through to selection clearing.
— an exported pure helper that answers "may this object be dragged or resized?": true when the object itself carries
(either mode), or when the walk up its
. The walk is cycle-safe — a visited set plus an iteration cap bounded by the document's object count — so a corrupt parent cycle degrades to "not locked" rather than hanging the pointer pipeline.
Move events during active gestures are rAF-coalesced in the drag pipeline.
any pending move before stepping the end event, and edge-pan re-steps feed the same coalescer so viewport-following drags still commit at most once per frame.
is the host-initiated cancel path used by Escape-key handling elsewhere in the editor: given the current
, it restores the pre-gesture geometry for an in-progress
) and returns to idle — the same restore path a pointercancel event triggers, just reachable without a corresponding pointer event.

props.title: The interaction core is a pure state machine, decoupled from React/DOM

text.insert: takes only normalized pointer events and plain data (document, selection, tool, viewport) as input, and returns only data (next state, actions, overlay) as output — it never touches the DOM, React state, or refs directly. This buys two concrete things. First, gesture logic that would otherwise require a real browser and pointer-event simulation — most notably the connection cascade resolved by

during a connector drag, and section drop-targeting during a move — becomes unit-testable with plain objects and a fake clock/scheduler, which is exactly what the interaction test suite below exercises. Second, and more fundamentally, it means every

the editor ever dispatches can originate from either a live pointer gesture stepped through this machine, or from external automation (tests, scripted samples, an agent) constructing the same actions directly — both paths converge on the identical reducer the model layer exposes, so there is exactly one way a document ever actually changes. This mirrors the model layer's own single-reducer decision: the interaction core is the pointer-driven producer of actions, but never a second mutation authority alongside it.
describes that reducer and its action vocabulary in full.
computes alignment guides for dragged/resized objects against other objects' edges and centers.
compares each of the moving bounds' three lines per axis (near edge, center, far edge) against the same three lines on up to

(100) nearest candidates — siblings sharing the dragged set's parent, plus every section regardless of nesting — within a caller-supplied screen-space threshold (6px, divided by zoom so the snap feels constant at any zoom level, per
). The closest match per axis wins; if multiple candidates tie at that closest distance on the same guide line, their spans merge into one visual guide.

separately detects equal-gap runs of objects along an axis and surfaces a spacing hint chip when the moving object touches one end of an equal-gap group — a FigJam-style "these three are evenly spaced" affordance. Grid alignment is handled by
) only applies when the drag started grid-aligned; otherwise values round to clean integers so objects avoid sub-pixel drift, a PD5-era fallback rule.
composes point/edge alignment with the ported AFFiNE equal-spacing ("distribution") snap —

— using AFFiNE's own priority rule: on a given axis, point alignment wins if it produced a non-zero correction, and the distribution search only runs (and can win) on an axis where alignment found nothing. This is the function
when it wants alignment and distribution snapping composed identically.
implements an in-memory clipboard — a module-level
captures the current object selection plus every descendant (via
, so copying a section brings its children along), plus every connection whose both endpoints land inside the captured set, deep-cloned via
(the copied selection's bounds center) used later to compute the paste offset. The editor's hotkey handler (
) stores the result in the module singleton via
on Cmd/Ctrl-C. On paste, it reads the payload back with

, which re-clones the objects and connections and translates every geometry — and every explicit connection waypoint, since W4 waypoints are stored as absolute world coordinates — by the delta between a target drop point (or a default 24-unit offset when pasting without one) and the recorded
, so a fan of connectors pastes intact rather than re-routing from scratch. The resulting objects and connections are dispatched in a single
action, so the whole paste — however many objects and connections it contains — lands as one reducer call and one undo step.
) and the pointer sits inside a
-px region near a stage edge, so the user can drag past the visible viewport bounds without releasing the pointer. Its sole export,
, takes the pointer's stage-relative position, the stage's screen-space size, the edge band width, and a max speed, and returns a
delta to add to the viewport's pan each animation frame (matching
). Each axis is computed independently, so a pointer parked in a corner yields nonzero
simultaneously. Velocity ramps linearly from 0 at
px away from the edge up to
right at the edge (or beyond, if the pointer has left the stage bounds entirely); a pointer more than
px from every edge — the "safe" interior — or a non-positive
exercises this layer purely at the data level, with no browser:
covers the general gesture set (press/click/drag thresholds, move, resize, drag-select, place), plus connector endpoint drag, connector bend drag, and connector create;
remains cascade-specific for connector create/reconnect candidate resolution against the AFFiNE cascade (anchor/outline/inside priority);
with a fake scheduler to verify collapse-to-latest, synchronous flush, and cancel-without-commit;
injection hook overrides the default snap computation during a move gesture without ever affecting cancelled-drag restoration;
cover alignment-guide and equal-spacing/distribution snap math respectively; and

's velocity ramp and corner/out-of-bounds cases. Because none of this layer touches the DOM, every one of these suites runs as fast, deterministic unit tests rather than browser-driven component tests.

Connector routing is closely related to this layer: connections get re-routed live during the move, resize, connector create/reconnect, and selected connector bend-drag gestures described above. Bend-drag preview uses routed
points and commits waypoints through the connector gesture stepper. See
for the connector routing and cascade-resolution behavior itself.
